coredata.dc:title: Formulation and evaluation of anti-osteoarthritic, cytotoxicity and antioxidant effects of carbon dots derived Allium sativum L. leaf extract
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University
coredata.dc:creator: He, Zhiyu
Yu, Jiaxi
Chen, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of carbon nanostructured material for biomedical application has gained a lot of interest in recent years. Herein, the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) using Allium sativum L. leaf extract were demonstrated using hydrothermal process. The CDs were characterized using various techniques including UV-Vis, DLS, FT-IR, XPS and TEM methods. The green synthesized CDs were mostly spherical with diameter of 2.1 ±0.6 nm with high colloidal stability. Good antioxidant activity versus DPPH was observed using the prepared CDs. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity effect of carbon CDs was investigated using MTT strategy. Moreover, the prepared CDs were applied as scavengers of reactive oxygen species in osteoarthritis (OA). For clinical applications, the green source CDs demonstrated positive effects in terms of antioxidant, anti-osteoarthritis, and cytotoxicity properties. The obtained results have the potential to offer fresh perspectives on the treatment of different inflammatory diseases caused by reactive oxygen species. Additionally, the CDs were used to determine Fe(III) by fluorescence (FL) procedure. The decrease of FL intensity allowed to measure Fe(III) in a wide linear range (10 nM-0.5 mM) with a low detection limit of 4.0 nM. These findings show that the prepared CDs have great potential for various applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antioxidant
Carbon dots
Leaf extract
Green protocol
Osteoarthritis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CDs. Inset depicts corresponding hydrodynamic diameter analysis.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) UV-Vis absorption data, (c) XPS of CDs and (d) high-resolution C 1 s data for prepared CDs via Allium sativum L. leaf extract.

(a) FL peaks of CDs in absence (red) and presence (yellow) of 0.5 mM Fe(III) and (b) FL spectra of CDs at various concentrations of Fe(III). Inset depicts plot of FL intensity decrease (F0-F) versus the logarithm of concentrations of Fe(III).
Cytotoxicity activity of synthesized CDs versus HUVEC cells.
Antioxidant property of formed CDs via Allium sativum L. leaf extract.
Analysis of inflammatory factors after treatment with CDs.
Comparison of the suggested assay and recently reports for FL determination of Fe(III).
Fe(III) analysis in human serum sample by green prepared CDs.

head.ce:title: Formulation and evaluation of anti-osteoarthritic, cytotoxicity and antioxidant effects of carbon dots derived Allium sativum L. leaf extract

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Orthopedics, The Affiliated Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital of Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Orthopedics, The Affiliated Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital of Southwest Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Acupuncture and Moxibustion Rehabilitation Department, The Affiliated Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital of Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichua 646000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Acupuncture and Moxibustion Rehabilitation Department, The Affiliated Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital of Southwest Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Acupuncture and moxibustion rehabilitation department, The Affiliated Traditional Chinese Medicine Hospital of Southwest Medical University, Luzhou 646000, Sichua, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of carbon nanostructured material for biomedical application has gained a lot of interest in recent years. Herein, the preparation of carbon dots (CDs) using Allium sativum L. leaf extract were demonstrated using hydrothermal process. The CDs were characterized using various techniques including UV-Vis, DLS, FT-IR, XPS and TEM methods. The green synthesized CDs were mostly spherical with diameter of 2.1 ±0.6 nm with high colloidal stability. Good antioxidant activity versus DPPH was observed using the prepared CDs. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity effect of carbon CDs was investigated using MTT strategy. Moreover, the prepared CDs were applied as scavengers of reactive oxygen species in osteoarthritis (OA). For clinical applications, the green source CDs demonstrated positive effects in terms of antioxidant, anti-osteoarthritis, and cytotoxicity properties. The obtained results have the potential to offer fresh perspectives on the treatment of different inflammatory diseases caused by reactive oxygen species. Additionally, the CDs were used to determine Fe(III) by fluorescence (FL) procedure. The decrease of FL intensity allowed to measure Fe(III) in a wide linear range (10 nM-0.5 mM) with a low detection limit of 4.0 nM. These findings show that the prepared CDs have great potential for various applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, the field of materials science has experienced a groundbreaking advancement with the introduction of nanotechnology, which has led to the development of nanostructured materials possessing notable physicochemical properties [1–3]. In recent times, nanomedicine has gained extensive recognition and application in term of detecting and treating diverse diseases [4–6]. The application of nanomaterials has seen considerable expansion in the domain of tumor imaging, identification of cancer biomarkers, and precise administration of therapeutic medications [7–9]. Nanosized materials, ranging from 1 to 100 nm in size, display distinctive physicochemical characteristics, including a high surface to volume ratio, increased reactivity, and notable photocatalytic capabilities when compared to their bulk substances [10,11]. In recent years, carbon nanostructures received extensive attention on scientific research, particularly in the healthcare field, owing to their distinctive structural features [12–14]. Since their discovery in 2004, carbon dots (CDs) have been extremely researched and are considered part of the next generation of carbon compounds [15]. As the outstanding carbon nanomaterial, CDs show strong and bright fluorescence due to their particle size [16]. CDs have found applications in a wide range of areas, including medicine [17], electrochemical sensing/biosensing [18], photocatalysis [19], and fluorescence sensing [20], thanks to their excellent features. Unlike the high atomic weight metal and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are water-soluble, biocompatible with low toxicity [21]. Their small size and controllable surface functional groups contribute to their ability to form strong interactions with biological surfaces [22]. From above point of view, CDs can be employed in various biomedical areas including sensing, imaging, labeling, drug delivery and photodynamic therapy [23]. Besides, some recent researches have highlighted the antioxidant and antimicrobial capabilities of CDs against different types of microorganisms, including bacteria, fungi, and viruses [24,25]. This breakthrough has opened up new applications for the use of CDs in medicine.

Heretofore, various synthesis methods like arc discharge [26], laser ablation [27], electrochemical [28], sonochemical [29] and microwave irradiation [30] have been employed for CDs fabrication. Green synthesis approaches utilize natural products, providing a renewable and low cost alternative to conventional manufacturing methods for CDs fabrication [31]. In view of this, different renewable natural substances including juice, milk, peels, potatoes, hair, orange and plant extract have been successfully utilized for facile and green preparation of CDs [32–34].

As being well known, Fe(III) is a key part of heme groups and a common transition metal found in our bodies [35]. There are different methods for measuring Fe(III) such as flame atomic absorption spectroscopy [36], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [37] and UV spectrometry [38]. Most of the methods mentioned earlier necessitate advanced and costly equipment, complex sample preparation, and are time-consuming. On the other hand, fluorescent sensors provide an uncomplicated and inexpensive method for analyzing metal ions in biological and environmental samples [39].

Herein, for the first time we study the fabrication of CDs from garlic (Allium sativum L.) leaf extract with facile hydrothermal approach. The synthesized CDs were characterized by prevailing instrumentation methods like TEM, DLS, FTIR and UV-Vis. The anti-osteoarthritic, antioxidant and cytotoxicity properties of the CDs were explored in details. This research also confirms the outstanding anti-osteoarthritic and antioxidant potential for the bio-inspired CDs. In addition, the CDs were successfully applied for sensitive and selective detection of Fe(III) using fluorescence (FL) assay. The suggested strategy offers several benefits, such as an affordable and eco-friendly carbon source, a convenient and efficient synthesis process for CDs, and impressive biomedical capabilities.

All chemicals and solvents including 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), FeCl3, CuCl2, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2 and AgNO3 were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) or Merck (Germany). The UV-Vis absorption spectra of CDs were recorded using an UV-Vis spectrophotometer (U-3900, Hitachi, Japan) at room temperature. The FTIR analysis was performed with a FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet-6700, ThermoElectron, USA). TEM image was obtained using a JEOL microscope (JEM-2100, Japan). A Nano Zetasizer ZS/ZEN3690 (Malvern, England) was used for evaluation of size distribution. All FL tests were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer (Japan) using a quartz cell (1 cm×1 cm). Methylthiazolydiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) test was carried out using an ELISA plate reader (Synergy H1, BioTek, USA) at 490 nm of excitation and 530 nm of emission.

The collected fresh Allium sativum L. leaf were rinsed with tap water and distilled water several times for removing some impurities. The leaf samples were then dried until the moisture content was about 25%. In the next step, 5 g of the dried sample was ground to produce a uniform paste. Afterward, 100 mL of distilled water was added to above paste under agitation. The obtained mixture was heated at 80 °C for 1 h, followed by natural cooling to the surrounding temperature. The obtained extract was filtered through Whatman filter paper (No. 1) to eliminate the residual impurities. The extract solution was preserved in a refrigerator (2–8 °C) until use for synthesizing CDs.

The facile hydrothermal approach was adopted for the preparation of CDs. In brief, the achieved extract was immersed in a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and administered to an oven for heating process at 180 °C. After 12 h, the resulting suspension was subjected to centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 5 min, followed by filtration using a 0.22 µm membrane. The suspension product was stored in a refrigerator (2–8 °C) for further application.

The DPPH free radical scavenger method was exerted to explore the radical scavenging effect in 96 well microtiter plates (MTP) based on reported method [40,41] with some modifications. In brief, stock solution of DPPH (2 mg/mL) was prepared in pure methanol solvent and then diluted to desired concentrations. Then, each well was treated with 5 μL of DPPH solution. After shaking for 3 min, the plate was stored at dark condition. As a control, a DPPH solution was used. An ELISA plate reader [42] was employed to record the optical density of the solution after 30 min. The ntioxidant activity is estimated by the percentage discoloration of samples. 3 replicate experiments were conducted in this study.

In order to evaluate the biocompatibility of prepared CDs, methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) method was utilized [43]. For this purpose, a 96-well culture plate containing DMEM medium was employed to seed cells. Firstly, 96-well culture plate was seeded with cells. After 2 h incubation, different concentration of CDs (0, 2, 4, 50, 8, 15, 30, 50, 75, 100 and 150 µg/L, pH 7.4) were subjected to the cells. After 20 h treatment at 37°C (with humidified 5% CO2), 20 μL of MTT reagent was added to each well. Afterwards, the plate was incubated for an additional 4 h. To dissolve formazan crystal, 100 μL of DMSO solvent was added to each well, followed by absorbance measuring with the help of ELISA plate reader. Without use of CDs, control tests were conducted under the same experimental conditions.

First, 50 μL of CDs (1 mg/mL) and 50 μL of Britton-Robinson buffer (pH 6.0) were added to a vial. Then, a suitable amount of Fe(III) working solution was added. The solutions were then diluted with distilled water to a total volume of 500 μL and mixed well. After incubating for 10 minutes, the mixtures were analyzed using a spectrophotometer to measure the fluorescence intensity.

Tests on animals were conducted according to the Animal Ethics Committee of the Animal Experiment Center of West China Hospital, Sichuan University (20220303074). The animals (C57BL/6 mice) were kept at 22 ±2 °C over the sterile paddy husk bedding under 12 h light/12 h dark cycles. In vivo studies were conducted on thirty female 7-week-old animals. An OA mouse model was induced by destabilizing the medial meniscus (DMM) [44]. In order to evaluate the inflammatory of OA, four weeks of intra-articular injection of CDs (0.2 mL of 20 mg/mL) were administered to the treatment group. Daily weighing was carried out during the treatment. The animals were anesthetized with tiletamine/zolazepam, and their serum samples were collected to explore the levels of expression of inflammatory factors consisting of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α using ELISA assay [45].
Statistical data were analyzed on a SPSS software program. Statistically significant differences between the blank and treatment categories were probed using Student’s t test at p< 0.01 [46].

The TEM image of the bio-inspired CDs is illustrates in Fig. 1. As seen, the prepared CDs have well spherical morphology. The hydrodynamic diameter analysis (Inset of Fig. 1) also confirms the formation of CDs with particle size of 2.1 ±0.6 nm. In addition, after two months storing at 4±1 °C, the fabricated CDs demonstrates a negligible change in the average size.

Moreover, the FTIR spectrum of the CDs was measured to obtain further understanding of their structure. According to Fig. 2a, the broad band appearing at about 3300 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O−H groups. The strong band at 1770 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration band of CO in CDs structure. The bands at 1440, 1231 and 947 cm−1 are characteristics for the stretching of CC, C−C and C−O groups, respectively [47].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs is also depicted in Fig. 2b. π → π* and n → π* electronic transitions are responsible for the absorption peaks at 238 and 341 nm, respectively [48]. In order to provide a more detailed understanding of the components present in the current CDs, XPS survey spectra were conducted. The results, depicted in Fig. 2c, clearly showed the emergence of three significant peaks representing carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N). This indicated that the CDs prepared in this study primarily consisted of these three elements. Additionally, the spectrum of C 1 s displayed three distinct peaks at energy levels of 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288.0 eV (as shown in Fig. 3d). These peaks revealed the presence of C-C, C-O, and CO bonds on the surface of the CDs, providing further confirmation of the previous findings obtained through FT-IR analysis.

As depicted in Fig. 3a, the FL intensity of CDs was significantly reduced when Fe(III) was added to solution. This finding confirms that the FL of CDs is responsive to Fe(III) ions, making them suitable for use as a FL sensor in the detection of Fe(III). The decrease in FL intensity (F0-F) was plotted against the logarithm of Fe(III) concentration, revealing a linear range from 10 nM to 0.5 mM (Fig. 3b). The inset of Fig. 3b further demonstrates the linear relationship between (F0-F) and Fe(III) concentrations, with a linear regression equation of Y=-153.59X+1199.6 and R2 value of 0.9923. The precision of this FL probe was also assessed, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.1% for five repeated measurements of 200 nM, indicating excellent precision. The detection limit for Fe(III) was determined to be 4 nM according to S/N=3. To compare the effectiveness of this assay, Table 1 presents a comparison with other fluorescence sensors reported for Fe(III) detection [49–52]. The results show that the detection limit of this assay is below to previously reported methods for Fe(III) analysis. The selectivity of suggested methodolory towards F(III) was studied by testing the response to other metal ions (1000 nM for Ni(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Cu(II) and Ag(I)) in presence of 200 nM Fe(III). No significant interferences were observed when Fe(III) measured via the prepared CDs. The applicability of this analytical protocol was tested through standard recovery tests conducted in human serum samples ( Table 2). The results demonstrate satisfactory recoveries of 100.8%, 98.6% and 100.6% for human serum samples, with minimal interference from blood matrices. This indicates that the suggested sensor has the potential to be applied for the practical detection of Fe(III) in clinical applications.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the biogenically synthesized CDs was studied using MTT colorimetric method [53] to evaluate their biocompatibility. For this purpose, various concentrations of 0, 2, 4, 8, 15, 30, 50, 75, 100 and 150 µL/mL CDs were incubated 24 h with human umbilical vein endothelial cell (HUVEC) line. As illustrated in Fig. 4, CDs caused minimal toxicity on normal (HUVEC) cells. As a result, the fabricated CDs have the potential to be a safe and harmless material for various biological applications.

Fig. 5 illustrates the study of the free radical scavenging property of the fabricated CDs using the antioxidant DPPH approach. This platform has been widely recognized as a powerful technique to explore the ability of CDs to act as free radical scavengers [54]. As seen from Fig. 5, the prepared CDs depict a DPPH radical scavenging activity 66.4–4.5% for the studied concentrations ranging from 200 µg/mL to 2 µg/mL. Due to their excellent free radical scavenging properties, previous investigatins have considered the CDs as new antioxidant agents [55,56]. As a result of the present work, free radical scavenging capability for the formed CDs has also been confirmed. The antioxidant property of CDs is attributed to their ability to quench DPPH-free radicals.

To evaluate the anti-OA property of CDs, the levels of some inflammatory mediators like IL-1β, IL-6 and TNF-α after treatment with CDs were studied the animal with OA. For this purpose, the serum samples of mice which treated with biogenically synthesized CDs, were captured and the expression levels of inflammatory mediators were measured with ELISA analysis. According to the results ( Fig. 6), it was observed that CDs treatment had a notable influence on the IL-1β and IL-6 levels induced by OA. Besides, inflammatory cytokine (TNF-α) did not show any response to CDs treatment. The findings indicate that the use of CDs prepared via Allium sativum L. leaf extract can alleviate the symptoms of OA by suppressing the inflammatory response.

In present study, the highly stable CDs were successfully fabricated using a green source (Allium sativum L. leaf extract). The characterization data obtained from various techniques such as TEM, FT-IR, DLS, XPS and UV-Vis show that the prepared CDs have the spherical shape with particle size of 2.1 ±0.6 nm and the admissible functional groups. The significant antioxidant feature of the biogenically synthesized CDs was addressed through the DPPH radical scavenging experiments. In vivo studies have proved that the prepared material had satisfactory stability and could be employed as an effective carrier for OA treatment. Besides, the fabricated CDs indicated the minimal toxicity on normal human cell (HUVEC). Moreover, these CDs were employed as a novel sensor for the quantification of Fe(III) in biological fluid. The suggested Fe(III) detection method possess the advantages of good selectivity, short analysis time, low-cost and wide linear range. Based these findings, we conclude that the cost-effective bioinspired CDs can be useful for various biomedical and clinical applications owing to their unique properties.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances of nanomaterials for intervention in Parkinson’s disease in the context of anti-inflammation
